author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot of the feasible region for max 2x_1+5x_2 with vertices (0,0), (9,0), (6,5), (0,8), bounded by x_1+2x_2<=16 and 5x_1+3x_2<=45 (red); an inset table lists the objective value at each vertex (0, 18, 37, 40).

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot of the Furniture Workshop feasible region: a blue pentagon with vertices (0,0), (35,0), (35,10), (30,20), (0,30) bounded by 2x_1+x_2<=80, x_1+3x_2<=90, x_1<=35; dashed level curves 8x_1+6x_2 = 120, 240, 360 sweep upward.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot showing alternative optimal solutions: same Furniture Workshop pentagon with dashed level curves of 2x_1+x_2 (z = 20, 40, 60, 80). The z=80 curve overlays the entire edge from (30,20) to (35,10), highlighted as a thick blue segment.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot of an infeasible LP: a red triangle (x_1+x_2<=8) and blue triangle (2x_1+x_2<=12) sit lower-left while a vertical line x_1=7 (green) and horizontal x_2=5 (orange) require points upper-right; a No feasible region label fills the gap.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot of an unbounded feasible region for min 5x_1+7x_2 extending up and right, bounded by x_1+3x_2>=6 (red), 5x_1+2x_2>=10 (green), x_2<=4 (purple); dashed level curves of 3x_1+2x_2 mark the optimum at lower-left vertex (1.38, 1.54).

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot of an unbounded LP for max x_1+3x_2: blue feasible region extends infinitely up and right with vertices (0,2) and (10/3, 1/3); dashed level curves z = 3, 6, 9, 12, 15 keep intersecting the region, so the objective is unbounded.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot of the same unbounded region under min 3x_1+x_2. Dashed level curves z = 2, 4, 6, 8 sweep from the lower-left; the smallest curve meeting the region passes through (0,2), marked as the finite optimum with z* = 2.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Data table styled as a figure listing three dietary supplement products with cost and milligrams of nutrients M and N -- the input data for the diet LP (minimize cost subject to nutrient requirements).

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot of the LP feasible region for max -5x_1-x_2 with extreme points (0,0), (0,1), (2,3), shaded blue on a 0-5 grid. Lines s_1=0 (red), s_2=0 (teal), s_3=0 (orange) bound the region; vertices marked with blue dots and red squares.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot replotting the same feasible region (extreme points (0,0), (0,1), (2,3)) but with s_1=0 (red) and s_2=0 (teal) drawn as full plotted lines rather than just segments, to discuss extending the constraint lines.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot of the recession cone obtained by setting b=0: a thin blue triangular cone bounded by s_1=0 (red), s_2=0 (teal), s_3=0 (orange) emanating from the origin, with the line x_1+x_2=1 drawn from (0,1) to (1,0) across it.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot of the same recession cone with rays s_1=0, s_2=0, s_3=0 from the origin and the line x_1+x_2=1, used to isolate the line segment of extreme directions inside the cone.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot magnifying the segment x_1+x_2=1 inside the recession cone, showing extreme directions (4/5, 1/5) and (2/3, 1/3) as red boxes with blue dots; bounded by s_1=0 (red) and s_3=0 (orange), with redundant s_2=0 removed.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Illustration of Big-O notation: a red curve f(n) and blue curves g(n) and c times g(n) plotted against n, with a dashed vertical line at n_0. To the right of n_0 the scaled curve c times g(n) stays above f(n), showing f(n) = O(g(n)).

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Left: triangle with vertices A, B, C and side lengths a, b, c, annotated with the three triangle inequalities a + b >= c, a + c >= b, and b + c >= a. Right: a nearly flattened triangle where vertex C sits close to segment AB, so c is approximately a' + b', the tight case of the inequality.

author: {Ilijas Farah, Ken Kuttler, and Lyryx Learning Team \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}}
alt_text: Argand (complex-plane) diagram showing the complex number z = 3 + 2i plotted as the point (3, 2) in the first quadrant, with dotted guide lines to the real axis at 3 and the imaginary axis at 2.

author: {Ilijas Farah, Ken Kuttler, and Lyryx Learning Team \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}}
alt_text: Argand diagram showing z = a + bi = r e^(i theta) as a thick blue arrow from the origin into the first quadrant; the modulus r = sqrt(a^2 + b^2) labels the arrow length and theta marks the angle from the positive real axis.

author: {Ilijas Farah, Ken Kuttler, and Lyryx Learning Team \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot introducing polar coordinates: a point in the first quadrant labelled both (x,y) and (r,theta), reached by a blue ray of length r from the origin, with theta marked at the origin and dashed projections to the axes.

author: {Ilijas Farah, Ken Kuttler, and Lyryx Learning Team \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}}
alt_text: Two-dimensional diagram of a unit circle centered at the origin with x- and y-axes. A blue ray from the origin through the circle at (cos(theta), sin(theta)) shows how the angle theta determines the ray on which a point at radius r is found.

author: {Ilijas Farah, Ken Kuttler, and Lyryx Learning Team \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot showing the point with Cartesian coordinates (5*sqrt(3)/2, 5/2), corresponding to polar coordinates (5, pi/6), in the first quadrant with dotted blue guide lines dropped to the x- and y-axes.

author: {Ilijas Farah, Ken Kuttler, and Lyryx Learning Team \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot showing the point with Cartesian coordinates (-5*sqrt(3)/2, -5/2), corresponding to polar coordinates (-5, pi/6), in the third quadrant with dotted blue guide lines to the x- and y-axes.

author: {Ilijas Farah, Ken Kuttler, and Lyryx Learning Team \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot comparing the points (5*sqrt(3)/2, 5/2) in the first quadrant and (-5*sqrt(3)/2, -5/2) in the third quadrant, joined by a blue line through the origin, showing they are on opposite sides of the origin at the same distance.

author: {Ilijas Farah, Ken Kuttler, and Lyryx Learning Team \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}}
alt_text: Two-dimensional sketch illustrating how to graph r=f(theta): two rays from the origin at angles theta_1 (blue) and theta_2 (red), each with a marked dot at the corresponding radius f(theta_i), with small arcs near the origin labelling the two angles.

author: {Ilijas Farah, Ken Kuttler, and Lyryx Learning Team \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}}
alt_text: Two side-by-side 3D sketches on x, y, z axes for cylindrical coordinates: the left shows a cylinder of radius r with a horizontal cross-section circle at height z; the right adds a red point (x,y,z) on the cylinder and the angle theta from the positive x-axis.

author: {Ilijas Farah, Ken Kuttler, and Lyryx Learning Team \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}}
alt_text: Two side-by-side 3D sketches for spherical coordinates: the left shows a sphere of radius rho about the origin; the right shows the same sphere with a red latitude circle of constant phi, where phi is measured from the positive z-axis.

author: {Ilijas Farah, Ken Kuttler, and Lyryx Learning Team \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}}
alt_text: Three-dimensional axis frame (x, y, z) with a red ray of length rho from the origin to a point (x,y,z), the angle phi from the positive z-axis to the ray, the projection (x,y,0) in the xy-plane shown in blue, and the angle theta from the positive x-axis to that projection.

author: {Ilijas Farah, Ken Kuttler, and Lyryx Learning Team \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}}
alt_text: Three-dimensional x, y, z axis frame summarizing Cartesian, cylindrical (r,theta,z), and spherical (rho,phi,theta) coordinates for one point: a red ray rho to (x,y,z) with angle phi from the z-axis, and a blue ray r to (x,y,0) with angle theta from the x-axis.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot of the line y = 3x + 2 on x-y axes with a gridded background. Three labelled points (-1,-1), (0,2), and (1,5) lie on a blue line of slope 3 with y-intercept 2.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot of the line 2x + y = 4 on gridded x-y axes. A blue line passes through three labelled points (-1,6), (0,4), and (1,2), illustrating slope -2 and y-intercept 4.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot of the line 2x - 3y = 6 on gridded x-y axes. The blue line is drawn through its two intercepts, the labelled points (3,0) and (0,-2).

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot of the parametrically defined line x = 3 + 2t, y = 1 + t on gridded x-y axes. A blue line passes through three labelled points (3,1), (5,2), and (7,3) corresponding to t = 0, 1, 2.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot of the horizontal line y = 3 on gridded x-y axes. The blue line is drawn parallel to the x-axis through the labelled y-intercept (0,3).

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot of the vertical line x = -2 on gridded x-y axes. The blue line is drawn parallel to the y-axis through the labelled x-intercept (-2,0).

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot illustrating the slope of the line through (-2,3) and (4,-1) on x-y axes. A blue line connects the two labelled points; green arrows mark a vertical drop of -4 and a horizontal run of 6, giving slope -2/3.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot of the line through (1,2) with slope -3/4 on gridded x-y axes. A blue line passes through the labelled points (1,2) and (5,-1); green arrows indicate a vertical change of -3 and horizontal change of 4.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot of an equilibrium point. The blue upward supply line y = 3.5x - 14 and the red downward demand line y = -2.5x + 34 intersect at the labelled green point (8,14), with dashed lines dropping to Quantity = 8 and Price = 14.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot of a break-even point. The blue revenue line R = 5x and the red cost line C = 3x + 12 intersect at the labelled green point (6,30), with dashed lines marking Quantity = 6 and Cost/Revenue = 30.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Schematic of a convex combination on a 2D plane: two black points x and y are joined by a line segment, with a red point z placed one-third of the way from y to x to illustrate z = lambda*x + (1-lambda)*y for lambda = 2/3.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two side-by-side shapes contrasting a convex set (a tilted gray ellipse) with a non-convex set (a curvy gray blob with a notch); a chord between two interior dots leaves the second shape, demonstrating non-convexity.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot showing a halfspace: the line through (-1.5, 1.5) and (1.5, -1.5) splits the plane on x_1, x_2 axes, with the upper side shaded blue and labelled Halfspace.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot showing a polyhedron: a small green four-sided polygon with vertices near (0.5,0.5), (1.5,0.2), (1.2,-0.8), (0.2,-0.5), labelled Polyhedron, on x_1, x_2 axes.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot showing a ball: a red disk of radius 1 centered at the origin, labelled Ball, on x_1, x_2 axes -- an example of a convex set defined by sum of x_i^2 <= 1.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot showing a second-order cone: an upward orange triangular wedge with apex at (0,2) and base from (-1,0) to (1,0), illustrating {(x,t) : ||x||^2 <= t^2} on x_1, x_2 axes.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot of a blue ball overlapping a red six-sided polytope; the lens-shaped overlap is shaded green. A short segment between two interior points x and y illustrates that the intersection of two convex sets is convex.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: One-dimensional number line showing c^T z between c^T x and c^T y. Three dots mark min and max of {c^T x, c^T y} and the convex combination c^T z = lambda c^T x + (1-lambda) c^T y in between.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot of a quadrilateral polytope with vertices (0,0), (1,2), (3,2), (4,0). A green vertex v and two interior red points x and y each show short arrows v +/- epsilon*d, distinguishing perturbations that stay inside from those that leave the polytope.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot of an LP feasible region on a 0-5 grid: shaded blue polygon with extreme points (0,0), (0,1), (2,3), bounded by s_1=0 (red), s_2=0 (teal), s_3=0 (orange) lines; vertices marked with blue dots and red squares.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Undirected graph G with 18 vertices labelled A through R, drawn in a roughly grid-like layout. Edges connect adjacent letters plus several long-range pairs (e.g., F-N, K-P, L-R), illustrating the simplified street-map graph from the housing development.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: The same 18-vertex graph G (vertices A through R) redrawn with vertices scattered in a non-grid layout. The edge set is identical to the previous figure, illustrating that a graph's structure is independent of how it is drawn.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Schematic of Konigsberg's four landmasses drawn as labelled ellipses (North Bank, Island, East Bank, South Bank) with seven short line segments crossing between them representing the seven bridges of the puzzle.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Graph abstraction of the Konigsberg bridges: four circular vertices labelled NB, I, EB, and SB with seven edges connecting them, including two parallel arcs between NB and I and two between I and SB to represent the duplicate bridges (a multigraph).

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Small graph illustrating a loop: three filled-dot vertices form a triangle and a red self-loop attaches to the rightmost vertex, showing an edge that connects a vertex to itself.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Single isolated vertex with no edges, illustrating a vertex of degree 0 in the table of degree examples.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: A vertex with one edge attached, illustrating degree 1 in the table of degree examples.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: A vertex with two edges attached (forming a V), illustrating degree 2 in the table of degree examples.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: A vertex with three edges attached, illustrating degree 3 in the table of degree examples.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: A vertex with four edges attached (one to each diagonal direction), illustrating degree 4 in the table of degree examples.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Final Sorted Edges circuit on the four-vertex graph: edges AD, AC, BD, and BC are highlighted, forming the Hamiltonian circuit ACBDA with total weight 25 (not optimal; the optimum ACDBA has weight 23).

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Empty layout of ten Oregon cities (Portland, Salem, Corvallis, Seaside, Ashland, Newport, Astoria, Eugene, Bend, Crater Lake) drawn as isolated vertices, ready to have Sorted-Edges edges added one at a time.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Same Oregon-cities layout after Sorted Edges has added the four shortest edges: Seaside-Astoria (17), Salem-Corvallis (40), Salem-Portland (47), and Corvallis-Eugene (47).

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Oregon-cities graph after seven Sorted-Edges selections: the previous four edges plus Portland-Seaside (78), Newport-Eugene (91), and Ashland-Crater Lake (108). Salem and Corvallis already have degree 2.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Oregon-cities graph showing the completed Sorted-Edges Hamiltonian circuit: ten edges connect the cities in the tour Portland, Salem, Corvallis, Eugene, Newport, Bend, Ashland, Crater Lake, Astoria, Seaside, Portland (1241 miles).

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Weighted graph on six vertices A, B, C, E, F, G arranged left to right with fifteen edges (weights from 11 to 45). Reader is asked to apply the Sorted Edges algorithm to find a Hamiltonian circuit.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: A 3-by-4 grid graph with 12 vertices labelled A-D (top), E-H (middle), J-M (bottom). All grid edges are drawn; a path from A to M is highlighted in thick red, traversing A-E-F-G-L-M.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: The same 3-by-4 grid graph (vertices A-D, E-H, J-M) with a circuit highlighted in thick red, beginning and ending at vertex A by traversing A-E-J-K-F-B-A and similar back path, illustrating a cycle.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: A 3-by-4 grid graph with vertices A-D, E-H, J-M, but with the column-A horizontal edges deleted so that vertex A and the left column are isolated from the rest, illustrating a disconnected graph.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Weighted graph of five U.S. cities (LA, Seattle, Chicago, Atlanta, Dallas) shown as filled dots arranged across the plane, with all ten edges drawn and labelled with one-way airfares such as $70 (LA-Seattle), $150 (LA-Dallas), $85 (Atlanta-Dallas), $75 (Chicago-Atlanta).

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Initial-state diagram for Dijkstra's algorithm: seven blue circular nodes T, E, A, NB, MR, P, Y connected by weighted edges (e.g., T-A=20, A-NB=36, NB-Y=120, MR-Y=90); each node has a green-boxed tentative-distance label, all set to infinity except the destination T which is 0.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Weighted road network for the Yakima-to-Tacoma example: seven filled-dot vertices T (Tacoma), E (Eatonville), A (Auburn), NB (North Bend), MR (Mount Rainier), P (Packwood), Y (Yakima) with edges labelled by travel times in minutes (e.g., T-A=20, A-NB=36, NB-Y=104, MR-Y=96, P-Y=76).

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Yakima-to-Tacoma road network as before, now with the destination Y annotated [0] in red to mark it as the starting vertex of Dijkstra's algorithm with tentative distance zero.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Yakima example after Dijkstra step 2: tentative distances [104] at NB, [96] at MR, [76] at P are recorded in red next to each neighbour of Y; Y still labelled [0]; remaining vertices T, E, A are unlabelled.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Yakima example after the second iteration: P is the current vertex; E now has tentative distance [172] (via P), and Y is shown struck-through (visited); other labels [104] at NB, [96] at MR, [76] at P remain.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Yakima example after the third iteration: A has tentative distance [175] (via MR); P and Y appear struck-through (visited); NB still [104], MR [96], E [172].

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Yakima example after the fourth iteration: A's distance is updated to [140] via NB; NB, MR, P, Y all struck-through; E still [172].

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Yakima example after the fifth iteration: T receives tentative distance [160] via A; A, NB, MR, P, Y are struck-through, leaving E [172] still active. The shortest path Y-NB-A-T of 160 minutes is now determined.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: A 4-vertex spanning tree drawn as filled dots at corners of a small region, connected by three edges forming a Y-shape (top-left to top vertex, bottom-right to middle, top to bottom-right), illustrating one possible spanning tree on four vertices.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: A 4-vertex spanning tree with three edges arranged as a path: middle vertex linked to top-right, top-right linked to bottom-right, and bottom-right linked to bottom-left, showing another spanning-tree configuration on four vertices.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Composite figure of five small spanning trees with n = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 vertices arranged left-to-right; each tree has exactly n-1 edges, demonstrating that a spanning tree on n vertices uses n-1 edges.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: n = 3 case from the spanning-tree examples: three labelled vertices A, B, C connected by two edges (A-B and B-C) forming a path, with exactly n-1 = 2 edges.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: n = 4 case from the spanning-tree examples: a star tree with center B connected to leaves A, C, and D by three edges, illustrating n-1 = 3 edges on n = 4 vertices.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Five small spanning trees side by side on n = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 vertices. Yellow circular nodes labelled A through E are connected by exactly n-1 edges in each, supporting the lemma that a tree on n vertices has n-1 edges.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Single yellow circular node labelled A drawn in isolation, illustrating the base case of the induction proof: a tree with one vertex and zero edges satisfies |E| = n - 1 = 0.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Pseudocode block titled 'Kruskal's Algorithm (Descriptive Version)'. Initialize T to empty; repeatedly pick the cheapest unused edge, skipping any edge that would create a cycle; stop once T contains |V|-1 edges, forming a minimum spanning tree of G.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Symmetric 10-by-10 distance table for ten Oregon cities (Ashland, Astoria, Bend, Corvallis, Crater Lake, Eugene, Newport, Portland, Salem, Seaside) with rotated headers. Cells give pairwise driving distances in miles, e.g. Astoria-Seaside=17, Crater Lake-Eugene=453.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Map-like graph of ten labelled Oregon cities partway through Kruskal's algorithm: edges Salem-Corvallis, Salem-Portland, Seaside-Astoria, Corvallis-Newport, Corvallis-Eugene, and Seaside-Portland are drawn; Ashland, Bend, and Crater Lake remain unconnected.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Map-like graph of ten Oregon cities showing the completed Kruskal minimum spanning tree with nine edges. Final additions Ashland-Crater Lake (108 mi), Bend-Eugene (128 mi), and Eugene-Ashland (178 mi) connect every city; total cable length 695 miles.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Undirected weighted graph with six yellow circular vertices A, B, C, E, F, G arranged hexagonally. Every pair is joined by an edge labelled with its integer weight (e.g. AB=11, BG=13, AE=14, EF=16, AC=33). Input graph for the Kruskal exercise.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Python code listing 'Loading the graph into NetworkX' that builds the prior 6-vertex weighted graph in NetworkX: it imports nx, creates an undirected Graph, adds nodes A,B,C,E,F,G, and feeds the 15 weighted edges via G.add_weighted_edges_from(edges).

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Five-vertex graph with cities A, B, C, D, E as black dots, fully connected with dollar-cost edge labels (AB=4, AE=5, AC=8, DC=7, plus higher cross-edges). Edges AB, AE, AC, DC are thick red, marking the MST built by Prim's algorithm starting at A.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Same five-city phone-line graph (A, B, C, D, E) used to illustrate Prim's algorithm. Edges are labelled with dollar costs and the spanning tree edges AB, AE, AC, DC are drawn in thick red while the remaining inter-city edges are thin black.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Boxed figure for Prim's algorithm: 7-vertex graph with vertices a, b, c, d, e, f, g as red circles. The minimum spanning tree edges d-a, d-f, a-b, b-e, e-c, e-g are drawn thick red; remaining edges are thin black with integer weights.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Seven-vertex weighted graph for the shortest-path exercise. Vertices A, B, C, D, E, F, G in a roughly diamond layout with integer edge weights (AB=1, AC=4, BD=3, CD=2, DE=2, DF=4, EG=7, FG=6, plus chords). Goal: find the shortest path from A to G.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Airfare table giving one-way dollar fares between Seattle, Honolulu, London, Moscow, and Cairo (e.g. Seattle-Honolulu $159, Honolulu-London $830, London-Moscow $245, Moscow-Cairo $329). Used as input for building a weighted graph.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Small undirected graph with five black-dot vertices arranged in two rows, with four edges (square plus a diagonal extension). One of three example graphs presented side-by-side asking 'Which of these graphs are connected?'.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Small undirected graph with five black-dot vertices; edges form one triangle on the right plus a separate vertical segment on the left, leaving the graph in two components. One of three connectivity examples.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Small undirected graph with five black-dot vertices fully linked through a series of edges so all vertices belong to one component. The third option in the 'Which of these graphs are connected?' exercise.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Eight-vertex graph drawn on a 3-by-3 grid layout with three short horizontal segments stacked at top, middle, and bottom; the components are not all linked, illustrating a disconnected graph for the second connectivity question.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Eight-vertex graph on a 3-by-3 grid layout where a central vertex (1.5,1) has high degree, connecting most of the graph; one corner pair remains separated. Used for the second 'Which of these graphs are connected?' question.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Eight-vertex graph with edges forming a connected outer cycle plus several interior diagonals; the third option in the second connectivity exercise.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Weighted graph of a Eurail segment with seven nodes (Paris, Lyon, Bern, Frankfurt, Munchen, Amsterdam, Berlin). Edges are labelled with travel times in hours:minutes (e.g. Paris-Amsterdam 1:25, Lyon-Bern 3:50, Munchen-Berlin 5:45). Dijkstra exercise from Bern to Berlin.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Five-vertex graph with vertex (1,1) at the center linked to the four corners and a top-right corner; mixed odd and even degrees. One of three small graphs in the question 'Does each of these graphs have an Euler circuit?'.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Middle of three side-by-side graphs in a tabular for an Euler-circuit exercise: six vertices at (0,0), (2,0), (4,0), (1,1), (3,1), (2,2); edges form a fan from (2,0) plus a triangle (1,1)-(3,1)-(2,2). All degrees are even, so an Euler circuit exists.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Five-vertex graph featuring a multi-edge (two parallel edges drawn with bend left and bend right between the same pair of vertices) plus diagonal connections; given as a candidate Euler-circuit graph.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Five-vertex graph forming a roughly pentagonal shape with three chords; used as a candidate Euler-circuit graph in the second 'Does each of these graphs have an Euler circuit?' exercise.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Six-vertex graph drawn as two stacked triangles sharing edges, presented as one of the candidate graphs in the second Euler-circuit exercise.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Five-vertex graph forming a square A-B-C-D with one interior vertex connected to two opposite corners; the third candidate in the second Euler-circuit exercise.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Twelve-vertex 4-by-3 grid graph with horizontal, vertical, and a few diagonal edges; the exercise asks the student to eulerize the graph using as few edge duplications as possible and then find an Euler circuit.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Sixteen-vertex 4-by-4 grid graph with mostly perimeter edges plus a few interior verticals and one horizontal; the exercise asks the student to eulerize the graph using as few edge duplications as possible and then find an Euler circuit.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Eighteen-vertex graph laid out on an irregular grid representing a neighborhood street map; the city crew exercise asks the student to find an efficient inspection route by eulerizing the graph and then finding an Euler circuit.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Five-vertex graph with corners at (0,0), (2,0), (0,2), (2,2) plus a center node (1,1); edges form a triangle on the bottom and a Z-shape through the center, asking whether a Hamiltonian circuit exists.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Bipartite graph K_{3,3}: three black-dot vertices on the bottom row at x=0, 1.5, 3 connected to three on the top row at the same x-coordinates by all nine cross edges; asks whether a Hamiltonian circuit exists.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Seven-vertex graph drawn as a 3-by-2 lattice plus a top peak at (1,2); edges form the perimeter of the lattice with two interior diagonals and a triangle at the top, presented as a Hamiltonian-circuit candidate.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Eight-vertex graph: outer square at (0,0), (2,0), (2,2), (0,2) and inner square at (.7,.7), (1.4,.7), (1.4,1.4), (.7,1.4), with corresponding outer-corner-to-inner-corner spokes; presented as a Hamiltonian-circuit candidate.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Bipartite graph K_{3,2}: three vertices on the bottom row at x=0, 1.5, 3 each connected by an edge to two vertices on top at (0.75,2) and (2.25,2), six edges total; presented as a Hamiltonian-circuit candidate.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Six-vertex graph: vertex (1,0) sits at the center linked by five edges to (0,0), (2,0), (1,1), (1,-1), and (2,-1), with additional edges forming triangles above and below; presented as a Hamiltonian-circuit candidate.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Weighted graph on five labelled vertices A, B, C, D, E in a pentagonal layout, fully connected by ten edges with dollar-cost labels (e.g. CD=$5.6, CA=$5.1, BC=$4.3, AE=$4.4, CE=$4.0); used to plan a minimum-cost sonet ring.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Bipartite graph K_{3,3} drawn as three black-dot vertices on the bottom and three on top, connected by all nine crossing edges; the exercise asks for which n a graph built this way has Euler or Hamiltonian circuits.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Twelve-vertex 4-by-3 grid with horizontal edges weighted 2, 3, 2 and vertical edges weighted 1; asks the student to eulerize the graph in the most efficient way considering the edge weights.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Sixteen-vertex 4-by-4 grid with horizontal edges weighted 2, 4, 2 and vertical edges weighted 1; asks the student to eulerize the graph in the most efficient way considering the edge weights.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Pentagonal weighted graph on labelled vertices A, B, C, D, E with nine edges and integer weights (e.g. CD=26, AC=24, BC=44, AB=22, AE=30, DE=28); asks the student to eulerize it minimizing total added weight.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Pentagonal weighted graph on labelled vertices A, B, C, D, E with nine edges and small integer weights (e.g. EC=12, AE=9, DE=13, BE=4, CD=11, AC=8, AB=10, AD=5, BD=6); asks the student to eulerize it minimizing total added weight.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Twelve-vertex 4-by-3 grid graph with weighted horizontal, vertical, and two diagonal edges (e.g. weights 5, 6, 7, 4 horizontally; 3, 2, 1 vertically; diagonal (2,0)-(6,2)=3 and (2,2)-(6,0)=2); two vertices have odd degree, used to motivate the Chinese Postman Problem.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Four-vertex undirected graph with B at (0,0), C at (4,0), D at (2,2), A at (2,4) and five edges BC, BD, BA, CD, CA forming a kite; left panel of an example illustrating an Euler path CABDCB.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Same four-vertex kite graph (B, C, D, A) as the left panel, but with directed arrows along the Euler path CABDCB; edges are numbered 1 through 5 in the order they are traversed.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Six-vertex graph with vertices A, B, C, D, E, F in a roughly diamond layout and nine undirected edges; the example asks the student to find an Euler circuit such as ADEACEFCBA starting and ending at A.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Same six-vertex graph as the previous panel, redrawn with directed arrows tracing the Euler circuit AECABCFEDA and edges numbered 1 through 9 in traversal order.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Five-vertex graph with B(0,0), C(4,0), D(2,2), A(2,4), E(6,2) and edges CE, BD, BA, CD, CA, plus a bent edge from A to C; vertices D and E have odd degree, A and C have degree 4, B has degree 2, illustrating Euler's path-but-not-circuit theorem.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Eighteen-vertex housing-development graph with nodes A through R laid out on a roughly grid-like map of streets; reused from earlier in the chapter to ask whether an Euler circuit exists for the lawn inspector.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Same eighteen-vertex housing-development graph with the eight vertices of odd degree (D, B, G, N, O, H, J, P) highlighted as filled yellow circles, showing why no Euler circuit exists.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: The eighteen-vertex housing-development graph redrawn with every street represented by two parallel edges (one straight, one bent) so that every vertex now has even degree, illustrating that the snowplow's two-sides-of-the-street graph does have an Euler circuit.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Five-vertex graph with B(0,0), A(1,2), C(1,-2), E(3,-1), D(3,1) and six edges; the original input graph for the worked Fleury's algorithm example that finds Euler circuit ADEBDCA starting at A.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Undirected graph on five vertices A, B, C, D, E (second panel of Fleury's algorithm trace). Edge AD has been removed; remaining edges are B-D, B-E, A-C, C-D, D-E. Current vertex is D; circuit so far is AD.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Same five-vertex graph (third panel of Fleury's algorithm trace). Edges AD and DE have now been removed, leaving B-D, B-E, A-C, C-D. From E only one option remains, so the rest of the Euler circuit ADEBDCA is forced.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Undirected graph on seven vertices A, B, C, D, E, F, G drawn left-to-right with B/E above and C/F below. Eleven edges connect the vertices; the reader is asked to decide whether an Euler circuit exists and, if so, to find one.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Rectangular 4-by-3 grid graph (twelve vertices, seventeen grid edges, no duplicated edges shown). This is the original graph used to illustrate three different eulerizations in Example 11.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: First eulerization of the 4-by-3 grid: five red bend-left arcs duplicate edges in the middle row and adjacent rows, giving every vertex even degree using five extra edges so an Euler circuit becomes possible.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Second eulerization of the 4-by-3 grid using a different choice of five duplicated edges (red bend-left arcs spread across the top, middle, and bottom rows). It also uses five extra edges and yields an Euler circuit.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Third eulerization of the 4-by-3 grid using seven duplicated edges (red bend-left arcs concentrated around the middle column). It is a valid eulerization but uses two more duplicated edges than the previous two examples.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Small graph with four vertices A (top), D (center), B (lower-left), C (lower-right) and five edges forming a kite-like shape. Reader must eulerize the graph and then find an Euler circuit on the result.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: 4-by-3 grid graph with twelve vertices labelled A-D (top row), E-H (middle), J-M (bottom). Red edges trace the Hamiltonian circuit ABFGCDHMLKJEA, showing a circuit that visits every vertex once but does not use every edge.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Graph on five vertices A, B, C, D, E with vertex E attached to C by a single edge. Because every walk reaching E must return through C, no Hamiltonian circuit exists; Hamiltonian paths such as ECDAB and ECABD do exist.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Weighted complete graph on five cities (Home/Seattle, LA, Chicago, Atlanta, Dallas) with all ten edges labelled by airfare in dollars (e.g., LA-Seattle $70, Chicago-Atlanta $75, Atlanta-Dallas $85). Used to motivate the Traveling Salesman Problem.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Weighted complete graph on four vertices A (top), D (center), B (lower-left), C (lower-right). Edge weights: AD=1, AC=2, AB=4, CD=8, BD=9, BC=13. Used to demonstrate the Brute Force algorithm for the TSP.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Tree diagram of all 24 routes through five cities for the Brute Force TSP. Root is Home (Seattle); next level branches to LA, Dallas, Chicago, Atlanta; subsequent levels enumerate the 4*3*2*1 orderings of the remaining cities.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Same four-vertex weighted graph (A top, D center, B lower-left, C lower-right; weights AD=1, AC=2, AB=4, CD=8, BD=9, BC=13) revisited to apply the Nearest Neighbor Algorithm.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Five-city air travel graph (Seattle, LA, Chicago, Atlanta, Dallas) with the NNA tour Seattle-LA-Chicago-Atlanta-Dallas-Seattle highlighted in red. The highlighted total cost is $450, which happens to be optimal here.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Same four-vertex weighted graph (A, B, C, D with weights 1, 2, 4, 8, 9, 13) used again to illustrate the Repeated Nearest Neighbor Algorithm running NNA from each starting vertex.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Sorted Edges algorithm step 1 on the four-vertex graph: only the cheapest edge AD (weight 1) is highlighted in red.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Sorted Edges step 2 on the same four-vertex graph: edges AD (weight 1) and AC (weight 2) are now highlighted in red.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Sorted Edges rejected case (BAD) on the four-vertex graph: adding edge AB to the previously selected AD and AC would give vertex A degree 3, so this third edge is rejected.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Sorted Edges second rejected case (BAD) on the four-vertex graph: adding edge CD to AD and AC would close the partial circuit ACDA without including vertex B, so CD is rejected.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Sorted Edges accepted case (GOOD) on the four-vertex graph: edge BD (weight 9) is added to AD and AC because it neither creates a degree-3 vertex nor closes a sub-circuit.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Branch-and-bound tree for a maximization integer program. The root node shows the LP relaxation with fractional solution x = (1.33, 5.167) and bound 16.664. Branching on x_1 gives a left child (x_1 <= 1, solution (1, 4.9), bound 16.5998) and a right child (x_1 >= 2, solution (2, 4.5), value 12, marked Suboptimal). Branching the left child on x_2 gives a node with integer solution (0, 4) and value 16, marked Integer, and an infeasible node (x_2 >= 5), marked Infeasible. Each node includes a small picture of the LP feasible region. The optimal solution is x = (0, 4) with value 16.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Directed graph on seven nodes labeled A through G where every node has exactly one incoming and one outgoing arc, but the arcs form two separate subtours (one through A, B, C and one through D, E, F, G) rather than a single tour.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Directed graph on the same seven nodes A through G where every node again has one incoming and one outgoing arc, and the arcs now form a single tour visiting all seven nodes. Both assignments use the same number of arcs, illustrating why subtour elimination constraints are needed.

author: {Ilijas Farah, Ken Kuttler, and Lyryx Learning Team \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}}
alt_text: Two-dimensional diagram of a rotation T applied to vectors u and v: cyan arrows show u, v, and their sum u+v with the dotted parallelogram completion, and blue arrows show the rotated images T(u), T(v), and T(u)+T(v), illustrating that T preserves vector addition.

author: {Ilijas Farah, Ken Kuttler, and Lyryx Learning Team \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}}
alt_text: Two-dimensional unit circle showing rotation by angle theta: cyan basis vectors e_1, e_2 on the x- and y-axes, and blue rotated images R_theta(e_1) = (cos theta, sin theta) and R_theta(e_2) = (-sin theta, cos theta), with theta marked at the origin.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Schematic of a two-period production planning network: two gray circles labelled 1 and 2. Up-arrows show production x_1, x_2; down-arrows show demands 10 and 4; horizontal arrows show inventory s_0 in, s_1 between nodes, s_2 out. Rows labelled Production, Inventory, Demand.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Schematic of a multi-period production network: gray circles labelled 1, 2, ..., T-1, T. Up-arrows show production x_t; down-arrows show demand d_t; horizontal arrows show inventory s_0 in, s_1, ..., s_{T-1} between nodes, s_T out. Rows labelled Production, Inventory, Demand.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Small icon: a stick figure (head, body, arms, legs) inside a blue-bordered circular node, labelled P_1. Used as the visual marker for an agent in the assignment-problem bipartite diagram.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Small icon: a stick figure (head, body, arms, legs) inside a blue-bordered circular node, labelled P_3. One of three identical agent markers used in the assignment-problem bipartite diagram.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Small icon: a clipboard-style rectangle with two horizontal lines inside a red-bordered task node, labelled T_1. Used as the visual marker for a task in the assignment-problem bipartite diagram.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Small icon: a clipboard-style rectangle with two horizontal lines inside a red-bordered task node, labelled T_2. One of three identical task markers used in the assignment-problem bipartite diagram.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Small icon: a clipboard-style rectangle with two horizontal lines inside a red-bordered task node, labelled T_3. One of three identical task markers used in the assignment-problem bipartite diagram.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Bipartite diagram for the assignment problem: three blue agent nodes P_1, P_2, P_3 on the left connected by edges to three red task nodes T_1, T_2, T_3 on the right, forming a complete bipartite graph K_{3,3} of all possible agent-to-task assignments.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: An undirected graph G with vertex set V = {1,2,3,4,5,6} and edges connecting vertices: (4,6), (4,5), (5,1), (1,2), (2,5), (2,3), (3,4). The graph shows 6 circular nodes arranged in a roughly pentagonal shape with node 6 extending upward, connected by line segments representing the edges.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: A directed graph (digraph) G with vertex set V = {1,2,3,4,5,6} and arcs (directed edges): (4,6), (6,4), (4,5), (5,1), (1,2), (2,5), (2,3), (3,4). The graph shows 6 circular nodes with arrows indicating edge direction. Note that (4,6) and (6,4) represent two separate arcs in opposite directions.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Three-layer transportation network drawn left to right: warehouses W1 (supply +30) and W2 (supply +20) connect by arcs to distribution centers D1 and D2, which connect to stores S1, S2, and S3 (demands 20, 15, and 15). Each arc is labeled with its per-unit shipping cost, for example W1 to D1 costs 4 and D2 to S3 costs 2.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Directed network with eight nodes labeled a through h. Node a has supply +10 and node d has supply +5; nodes b, g, and h have demands of 5 each. Each arc carries two labels: its cost in red and a flow amount in blue, illustrating the data of a minimum-cost flow problem.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Directed network from a green source node s to a red sink node t through intermediate nodes a, b, c, d, and e. Each arc is labeled in orange with its capacity, for example s to a has capacity 4, s to c capacity 8, and d to t capacity 7: the input data of a maximum-flow problem.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Directed max-flow network with optimum shown: green source s (left), red sink t (right), intermediate nodes a, b, c, d, e. Arcs are labelled with capacity (orange) and optimal flow (bold black); total source outflow equals 12.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: The same s-to-t capacitated network with a wide gray diagonal band drawn across it, marking an s-t cut. The band crosses four arcs whose capacities 2, 1, 4, and 5 are circled, illustrating that the cut capacity 2 + 1 + 4 + 5 = 12 bounds the maximum flow.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Disaster-relief transportation network: warehouse rectangles W1-W5 (inventories 200, 300, 150, 250, 180), distribution circles D1, D2, D3, and a central diamond Hub. Directed arcs labelled with capacities route supplies through distribution centers to the Hub.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Directed graph for a max-flow exercise: green source s (left), red sink t (right), gray intermediate nodes v_1..v_4. Arcs labelled with capacities (8, 11, 11, 4, 12, 15, 4, 7, 1, 10) along paths through the two layers, with cross-arcs between v_2 and v_1.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Directed graph for a min-cost network-flow exercise: six gray nodes v_1..v_6 with blue supply/demand labels (6, -5, -3, -6, -2, 10). Each arc carries two numbers, capacity in black and unit cost in red (e.g., 8/7, 11/4, 12/9, 15/2, 4/2, 7/8, 4/1, 11/7, 1/2, 10/1).

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Shaded feasible region in the plane with number of tables x on the horizontal axis and number of chairs y on the vertical axis, bounded by two blue constraint lines with marked corner points. Gray dashed parallel lines show level sets of the revenue objective sweeping across the region.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Shaded feasible region for the furniture production problem in the tables-chairs plane, bounded by two blue constraint lines that intersect near (10, 7), with no objective level lines drawn.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Feasible region for the furniture problem with red dashed revenue level lines and a darker shaded subregion showing the points where revenue is at least 70 percent of its maximum value; the darker region is a strip along the upper-right boundary of the feasible set.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Shaded feasible region in the tables-chairs plane bounded by two blue constraint lines with marked corner points, with gray dashed revenue level lines sweeping across the region (same picture as the earlier revenue-constrained figure).

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Two-by-two flowchart connecting four boxes: the Original LP (min or max, unrestricted variables, inequalities) maps by variable transformations to a Transformed LP in canonical form (max, all equalities, nonnegative variables); solving gives a Solution to the Transformed LP, which maps back to a Solution to the Original LP by reversing the substitutions, for example x_j = x_j^+ - x_j^-.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Flowchart titled 'When can you use canonical form?'. From the Original LP, one branch applies when all constraints are Ax <= b with b >= 0: add slack variables to reach standard form, giving a canonical form with an obvious basic feasible solution. The other branch applies when some constraints are equalities or have negative right-hand sides: add artificial variables and solve an auxiliary Phase I problem to find a starting BFS.

author: {Jeffrey Humpherys, Tyler J. Jarvis, and contributors, Brigham Young University \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0/us/}{CC BY 3.0 US}.}
alt_text: A three-dimensional feasible region (polytope) for a linear program, showing the convex polyhedron formed by the intersection of half-spaces that define the constraints. Vertices of the polytope represent basic feasible solutions.

author: {Robert Hildebrand}
alt_text: Central picture of a two-variable feasible region: a blue shaded polygon on a coordinate grid with red constraint lines and vertices at (0,0), (0,8), (6,5), and (9,0). Around it, four boxes show the same LP rewritten from the perspective of the basic feasible solution at each vertex, and a table lists the objective value at each vertex: 0, 40, 37, and 18, with the optimum 40 at (0,8).

author: {Ilijas Farah, Ken Kuttler, and Lyryx Learning Team \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot of three iterates of a dynamical system: dots at approximately (28, 27), (29, 28), and (29.5, 29) on x- and y-axes scaled to show values 28-30, illustrating how the orbit converges toward the fixed point (30, 30).

author: {Ilijas Farah, Ken Kuttler, and Lyryx Learning Team \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}}
alt_text: Plot of the level curve 6 x_1^2 + 4 x_1 x_2 + 3 x_2^2 = 7 in the x_1, x_2 plane: a tilted blue ellipse whose principal axes are rotated relative to the coordinate axes, illustrating the cross-term x_1 x_2.

author: {Ilijas Farah, Ken Kuttler, and Lyryx Learning Team \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}}
alt_text: Plot in the y_1, y_2 plane of the simplified level curve 7 y_1^2 + 2 y_2^2 = 7, drawn as an upright blue ellipse aligned with the new coordinate axes after diagonalization removes the cross term.

author: {Ilijas Farah, Ken Kuttler, and Lyryx Learning Team \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}}
alt_text: Plot of the level curve 5 x_1^2 - 6 x_1 x_2 + 5 x_2^2 = 8 in the x_1, x_2 plane: a tilted blue ellipse whose principal axes are rotated 45 degrees from the coordinate axes due to the x_1 x_2 cross term.

author: {Ilijas Farah, Ken Kuttler, and Lyryx Learning Team \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}}
alt_text: Plot in the y_1, y_2 plane of the diagonalized level curve 2 y_1^2 + 8 y_2^2 = 8, shown as a horizontally elongated blue ellipse aligned with the new principal axes.

author: {Ilijas Farah, Ken Kuttler, and Lyryx Learning Team \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}}
alt_text: Schematic of a flat airplane wing drawn as a cyan parallelogram tilted at angle theta to the horizontal, with chord A and span B labelled and a horizontal arrow showing wind of speed V blowing against the leading edge.

author: {Ilijas Farah, Ken Kuttler, and Lyryx Learning Team \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}}
alt_text: Three side-by-side 2D plots showing how two lines can intersect: 'One Solution' (crossing at a single point), 'No Solutions' (parallel lines), and 'Infinitely Many Solutions' (a single line representing two coincident equations).

author: {Ilijas Farah, Ken Kuttler, and Lyryx Learning Team \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}}
alt_text: Two-dimensional plot of the system x+y=3 and y-x=5: a green line and a blue line crossing at the labelled point (x,y) = (-1,4), with helper lines dropped to the x- and y-axes to show the unique solution.

author: {Ilijas Farah, Ken Kuttler, and Lyryx Learning Team \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}}
alt_text: Two side-by-side two-dimensional plots showing three lines in the plane: on the left, three lines that pairwise intersect but share no common point ('No Solution'); on the right, three lines all passing through a single marked point ('One Solution').

author: {\copyright~Robert Hildebrand \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}.}
alt_text: Layout of districts showing numbered regions and possible locations for fire stations.

author: {\copyright~Robert Hildebrand \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}.}
alt_text: Set cover representation of fire station problem showing which districts are covered by each potential fire station location.

author: {\copyright~Robert Hildebrand \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}.}
alt_text: Graph representation of fire station problem showing nodes connected to chosen fire station nodes by edges.

author: {Robert Hildebrand \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}.}
alt_text: Same contour-and-feasible-region scene: ellipse contours with starred minimizer, blue half-plane g1(x) <= 0, orange disk g2(x) <= 0. The point x-bar lies where only the half-plane constraint is active; arrows show grad g1 pointing outward and grad f pointing downward. The negative objective gradient is not a nonnegative multiple of grad g1, so the KKT conditions fail and the point is not optimal: sliding along the boundary improves the objective.

author: {\copyright~Robert Hildebrand \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}.}
alt_text: Same contour-and-feasible-region scene with the point x-bar on the boundary of the orange disk, where only g2(x) <= 0 is active. Arrows show grad g2 pointing outward from the disk and grad f pointing into the feasible region. The negative objective gradient does not lie in the cone generated by grad g2, so the KKT conditions fail and x-bar is not optimal.

author: {Robert Hildebrand \href{https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/}{CC BY-SA 4.0}.}
alt_text: Contour ellipses of an objective function with its unconstrained minimizer marked by a star, and a feasible region formed by a blue half-plane g1(x) <= 0 intersected with an orange disk g2(x) <= 0. At the boundary point x-bar both constraints are active; arrows show the constraint gradients grad g1 and grad g2 pointing outward and the objective gradient grad f pointing away from the star. The negative objective gradient lies inside the cone spanned by the two active constraint gradients, illustrating a point where the KKT conditions hold.
